serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of stir-fried bitter gourd with pork as a side dish in a Singaporean meal is approximately 150 g, representing one shared-plate portion alongside rice.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Bittergourd with Pork — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Stir Fried Bittergourd with Pork (cr_id F14080036). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Bitter Gourd and Low-Carbohydrate Vegetable Dishes — analogous estimate based on bitter gourd GI data from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried bitter gourd with pork as a composite dish. Bitter gourd (bitter melon) has a reported GI of approximately 32 (low) in the University of Sydney GI Database, reflecting its very low carbohydrate content and hypoglycaemic phytochemical profile. Given that carbohydrate content of this dish is only 0.88 g/100g, the GI of the composite dish is estimated at 32 (low), consistent with the bitter gourd anchor. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (32 × 0.88 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 0.4, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 371 mg/100g — borderline for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 371 mg/100g is below the 400 mg/100g threshold but is meaningful at a 150 g serving (≈556 mg sodium); individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g and request reduced soy sauce and fermented black bean quantities to keep total sodium contribution within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (0.88 g/100g) with bitter gourd hypoglycaemic properties
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 0.88 g carbohydrate and 2.46 g dietary fibre per 100 g, this dish has a negligible glycemic load; bitter gourd's bioactive compounds (charantin, polypeptide-p) may provide additive blood glucose-lowering effects, so individuals on insulin or sulfonylureas should monitor for hypoglycaemia when consuming large portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated fat (14.1 g/100g) including saturated fat from pork, and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides 14.1 g total fat per 100 g with an estimated 4.8 g saturated fat, which contributes to daily saturated fat limits; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 100 g, choose leaner pork cuts, and be mindful of cumulative sodium intake from soy sauce and fermented black beans.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork (red meat) contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pork as a primary ingredient is a source of arachidonic acid that can amplify pro-inflammatory eicosanoid production relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; substituting pork with chicken breast, tofu, or fish would substantially reduce the inflammatory load while retaining bitter gourd's anti-inflammatory and antioxidant benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Bitter Gourd with Pork (苦瓜炒猪肉) is a classic Singaporean Chinese home-cooked dish in which sliced bitter gourd (bitter melon, Momordica charantia) is stir-fried together with sliced pork over high heat with aromatics such as garlic, fermented black beans (douchi), and soy sauce until fragrant and just cooked through. The dish is characterised by the distinctive bitter, slightly astringent flavour of the gourd balanced by the savoury umami depth of fermented black beans and the richness of pork. The texture contrasts tender-crisp gourd slices with soft, juicy pork. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (10.3 g/100g) and fat (14.1 g/100g) from pork, while carbohydrate content is extremely low at 0.88 g/100g, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. Dietary fibre is meaningful at 2.46 g/100g, contributed primarily by the bitter gourd. Sodium is 371 mg/100g, reflecting the use of soy sauce and fermented black beans. Bitter gourd contains bioactive compounds including charantin, polypeptide-p, and vicine that are associated with hypoglycaemic effects, making this dish of particular interest for blood glucose management. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to pork content, and contains soy allergens from soy sauce and fermented black beans. It is typically served as a shared side dish alongside steamed white rice in a Singaporean or Chinese family meal context.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080036) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. The match is exact. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using bitter gourd as the primary carbohydrate-contributing ingredient, which is a defensible analogous estimate given the dish's negligible carbohydrate content.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at 4.8 g/100g based on typical pork belly / lean pork blend composition from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10006, pork, fresh, composite) as HPB source does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is an approximation and should be verified if detailed fatty acid data becomes available from HPB. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish (F14080036) and are not available from a single approved source for this exact composite preparation as of 2026-04-16. 3. Dietary tags in the input incorrectly listed 'vegetarian', 'plant_based', and 'keto'; vegetarian and plant_based have been removed as the dish contains pork; keto classification is noted as plausible given 0.88 g carbohydrate/100g but is not a formal dietary flag in this schema. 4. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate anchored to bitter gourd GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. 5. Halal flag is set to false as the dish contains pork.
